section: Tensile deformation behavior analysis of low density Fe–18Mn–10Al– xC steels

context_update.summary: This chunk contains the abstract and introduction of the paper. It introduces three low-density Fe–18Mn–10Al–xC steels with carbon contents of 0.5, 0.8, and 1.2 wt%. The abstract reports key microstructural and mechanical findings: 0.5C steel is duplex ferritic–austenitic with 59.2% austenite; 0.8C steel has 84.8% austenite and ordered phases in both phases; 1.2C steel is fully austenitic with κ carbides. Tensile properties: 0.8C exhibits highest tensile strength of 979 MPa; 1.2C shows UTS 946.7 MPa and 56% elongation with superior strain hardening. C-J analysis reveals two-stage hardening for 0.5C and 0.8C, three-stage for 1.2C. The introduction reviews prior studies on similar Fe–Mn–Al–C alloys, highlighting effects of C on strength and ductility, and the occurrence of ordered phases. Later chunks will need to retain these sample aliases and baseline microstructures and properties for understanding subsequent experimental methods, detailed microstructural evolution, and deformation substructure analysis. No figures or tables are presented in this chunk.
properties: 0.8C: tensile strength 979 MPa, moderate strain hardening
1.2C: UTS 946.7 MPa, EL 56%, superior strain hardening
mechanisms: C-J analysis: two-stage hardening in 0.5C and 0.8C, three-stage in 1.2C

context_update.summary: This chunk covers the experimental procedure and the initial results for Fe–18Mn–10Al–xC steels with nominal 0.5, 0.8, 1.2 wt% C. Processing includes forging at 1200 °C, homogenization for 3 h, hot rolling to 3.5 mm, annealing at 1000 °C for 1 h, cold rolling to 1 mm, and a final 1000 °C anneal with water quench. Measured compositions (Table 1) and calculated densities (6.92, 6.88, 6.78 g/cm³) are reported. As-quenched microstructures: 0.5C and 0.8C are duplex ferrite–austenite with 59.2% and 84.8% austenite; 1.2C is fully austenitic with annealing twins. TEM shows no precipitates in 0.5C; 0.8C contains κ-carbide in austenite and B2+DO₃ in ferrite; 1.2C contains κ-carbide in austenite. Tensile properties: UTS max 979 MPa (0.8C), max elongation 56.2% (1.2C), 1.2C UTS×TE 53.2 GPa%. C–J analysis reveals two-stage hardening for 0.5C and 0.8C, three-stage for 1.2C. Deformation substructure evolution (Fig. 6–8) details planar slip, Taylor lattices, microbands, dislocation tangles, and superdislocations at specific strains. Subsequent chunks will need these phase fractions, ordered phases, hardening stages, and deformation structures to interpret mechanisms. Key visuals: Fig. 1 OM, Fig. 2 TEM, Fig. 3 stress-strain, Fig. 4 hardening rate, Fig. 5 C-J plots, Fig. 6–8 deformation TEM.
processing_route: Forging 1200 °C → homogenization 1200 °C 3 h → hot rolling to 3.5 mm (90% reduction) → annealing 1000 °C 1 h → cold rolling to 1 mm → annealing 1000 °C + water quench
key_variables: Strain hardening stages two and three
microstructure: Fully austenitic in 1.2C with annealing twins
mechanisms: Planar slip, Taylor lattice, microbands, dislocation tangles, paired superdislocations at various strains

context_update.summary: This chunk (section 3.3) presents tensile deformation microstructural evolution and discussion of mechanical properties and strain hardening. It includes Table 2 with tensile properties of 0.5C, 0.8C, and 1.2C steels (TE, YS, UTS, UTS×TE). Fig. 3 shows engineering stress–strain curves; Fig. 4 gives strain hardening rate vs. true strain; Fig. 5 displays C–J analysis ln(dσ/dε) vs ln ε curves, revealing two-stage hardening for 0.5C and 0.8C and three-stage for 1.2C. TEM observations (Figs. 6–8) detail dislocation structure evolution: in 0.5C, planar slip and dislocation tangles at low strain, then Taylor lattice, microbands, and dense tangles at higher strain; 0.8C exhibits single slip and effects of ordered phases; 1.2C undergoes planar slip, Taylor lattice, microbands, and microband interactions. Discussion attributes increased strength and ductility to ordered phases, strain partitioning, and extended hardening. Specific strength of 0.8C (142.3 MPa cm³/g) and comparison with literature alloys are noted. Subsequent chunks should reference these multistage hardening stages, the specific dislocation structures, and the interplay between carbon content, ordered phases, and hardening behavior.
microstructure: ordered B2 and DO₃ in ferrite of 0.8C
properties: 0.8C: TE 38.4%, YS 711.2, UTS 979.1 MPa, specific strength 142.3 MPa cm³/g
mechanisms: Two-stage work hardening (0.5C, 0.8C): stage 1 planar slip/dislocation tangles; stage 2 Taylor lattice/microbands plus more tangles
Three-stage hardening (1.2C): stage 1 planar slip; stage 2 Taylor lattice and microbands; stage 3 microband interaction
Ordered phases promote single slip; κ carbides enhance strength; microband interaction sustains hardening

context_update.summary: Section 4.2 concludes that Crussard–Jaoul (C–J) analysis effectively describes strain hardening in duplex, complex, and austenitic low-density Fe–Mn–Al–C steels, and that carbon content variations significantly affect microstructure-property relationships, allowing mechanical behavior control via alloying. Section 5 begins by restating the investigation scope: Fe–18Mn–10Al–xC alloys with 0.5, 0.8, and 1.2 wt% C. The specific main findings are listed as 'as follows' but not provided in this chunk. Expect subsequent chunks to present detailed conclusions on strain hardening, microstructure, and properties.
mechanisms: C-J analysis suitable for describing strain hardening in low-density steels
unresolved_terms: Main findings from conclusions not yet listed
